slides.description: The slide is titled "AI Screenplays" in large, bold blue text. Below the title is an illustration of a luxurious, wood-paneled office. A large window overlooks a sunset landscape featuring the 'HOLLYWOOD' sign on a distant hill. A man with grey hair, wearing a suit and red tie, stands behind a large wooden desk, gesturing with his right hand. To his left, on the desk, is a cardboard box robot labeled "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" with two large black circular eyes and a small rectangular mouth. On the left side of the desk, there are two golden Oscar-like statues, a golden Emmy-like statue, and a small red and gold trophy of a cartoon character with "CARTMAN" written on its base. A black desk phone is also on this side. In the center of the desk, in front of the man, are a brown journal, a document titled "SCRIPTS" in upside-down text, and a beige landline phone. To the right of the man, a globe rests on a stand next to a wooden cabinet. On the cabinet, a fish tank contains a green alien character. Above the cabinet, a framed picture on the wall displays the text "AWESOME-O" with two black circles for the O's, and the word "LAME" stamped in a red rectangle below it. A safe is visible next to the fish tank. On the desk in front of the man, a framed photograph shows a group of animated characters, including the man from the scene.
slides.narration: Alright, hey everyone! Welcome to the session. I'm your instructor for today, and, uh, we're gonna dive deep into some really cool stuff, you know, right here on our first slide: 'AI Screenplays.' You see this luxurious office, like, overlooking the Hollywood sign? And look at this guy, A.W.E.S.O.M.-O, chilling on the desk, surrounded by Oscars and Emmys. Don't mind that 'AWESOME-O LAME' picture on the wall, we're gonna make him *awesome* today, trust me. So yeah, today we're tackling one of the biggest challenges in AI: how do you get an AI to write a *long*, coherent document? I'm not talking about a paragraph or an email, I'm talking about a whole damn screenplay, or a book, or a lecture, right? Like, getting an AI to write small parts is easy, but keeping it focused for, say, 120 pages without it getting 'lost in the middle' or just, like, completely hallucinating and losing context? That's tough. But guess what? We figured it out. We're gonna show you a hierarchical, agentic AI architecture today – sounds fancy, I know – but it's basically a super smart way to break down this huge task into smaller, manageable chunks, handled by specialized AI agents. We call it our 'Screenplay Flow.' We'll walk through how these agents—like our Premise, Arc, Sequence, and Scene agents—each contribute, step by step, building on the previous one, to craft an entire narrative, all with structured outputs, you know, using JSON to keep everything tight. We'll even talk about 'enhancement agents' to make those intermediate outputs even better. And the *really* cool part? This isn't just for movies. You can literally generalize this entire framework, just by tweaking some agent prompts, to create, like, books, podcasts, songs, business plans, even other lectures. So yeah, we're gonna cover all of that, and then, um, we'll get into a hands-on coding session where you'll see it all come to life. It's gonna be pretty epic, I think. Let's get started!

slides.description: The slide is titled "Generating Long Documents" in large, bold blue text. Below the title are four bullet points. The first bullet states, "AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents." The second bullet provides examples: "Books, reports, screenplays." This directly relates to the previous slide's title "AI Screenplays." The third bullet explains, "Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies," reinforcing the theme of AI creating movie scripts introduced visually on the prior slide. The fourth bullet broadens the scope, stating, "This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents."
slides.narration: Okay, so, you know, just to really hammer home what we talked about on the last slide with A.W.E.S.O.M.-O and the Hollywood sign, this slide is basically our mission statement, right? 'Generating Long Documents.' The big takeaway is, yeah, AI *can* totally be used to generate these long, coherent text documents. We're talking books, reports, screenplays, like we said. And today, what we're actually going to *do*, is build that 'agentic flow' that I mentioned, specifically to generate screenplays for movies. That's our main example, you know, it's really fun to work with. But the super cool thing, and this is what we'll get to later, is that this exact same flow architecture, once you understand it, you can generalize it to pretty much any other long text document you can think of. So it's super versatile, like, you're learning something that's applicable way beyond just screenplays.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Challenge: "One-Shot" Long-Form" in large, bold blue text. This title introduces a specific difficulty in the generation of long documents by AI, building on the theme from the previous slide. Below the title are two main bullet points. The first bullet states, "It is difficult for an AI to generate a long coherent document in one-shot," directly defining the "one-shot" challenge. The second bullet elaborates on this difficulty, stating, "Local vs global – the AI is good at writing small parts, but cannot maintain coherence across whole document." This bullet has two sub-points: the first, "– Becomes “lost in the middle”," describes a common issue, and the second, "– Context drift – deep into the document, the AI can hallucinate or lose focus of what to write," explains another problem related to maintaining narrative or informational consistency over extended text.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've talked about what we're *going* to do, which is generate these long documents, let's talk about the *challenge*, right? This slide is literally titled 'Challenge: "One-Shot" Long-Form.' And this is the big hurdle. It is *really* difficult, like, for an AI to just spit out a whole novel, or a whole screenplay, or a whole lecture in one shot, you know? The core problem here is this 'local versus global' thing. The AI, it's *super* good at writing, like, a paragraph, or a small scene, right? It can handle those little parts beautifully. But when it comes to maintaining that coherence, that narrative thread, across a *whole* document – like, you know, 100 or 200 pages – that's where it just struggles. It just *can't* maintain coherence across the whole thing. And what happens? Well, it 'becomes lost in the middle.' You start a story, and the beginning is great, but by page 50, the AI is, like, 'Wait, what was I even writing about?' Or worse, you get 'context drift.' The deeper you get into the document, the more the AI can just start to hallucinate, or completely lose focus of what it's supposed to be writing about. It forgets characters, it introduces new plot points out of nowhere that make no sense with the beginning, um, it's just, like, a mess. So yeah, this is the problem we're trying to solve with our agentic flow.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Movie Screenplays" in large, bold blue text, continuing the focus on AI-generated screenplays from previous slides. The slide presents two main bullet points detailing elements and structure relevant to screenplays. The first main bullet point states, "Screenplays have many different elements the AI needs to capture." This bullet has three sub-points: "– Story shape", "– Goals of “sequences” in the movie", and "– Scene action and dialogue". The second main bullet point is "Hierarchical structure of a screenplay," which elaborates on how screenplays are organized, potentially addressing the "one-shot" challenge discussed in the previous slide. This bullet also has four sub-points: "– Premise – basic elements of the movie", "– Story arc – high level plot structure", "– Sequences – more detailed plot", and "– Scenes – even more detailed plot".
slides.narration: Okay, so, we just talked about the big, big challenge, right? That 'one-shot' long-form problem, where AI just gets totally lost in the middle. So, how do we tackle that for something as complex as a movie screenplay? Well, if you look at this slide, titled 'Movie Screenplays,' it actually gives us the blueprint, you know? First off, screenplays have *many* different elements that the AI needs to capture. It's not just, like, writing a story. You've got the overall 'story shape,' right? Like, the hero's journey, or whatever. Then you have the 'goals of sequences' in the movie – like, what's supposed to happen in Act 1, Act 2, and Act 3? And then, way down at the granular level, you have 'scene action and dialogue.' That's a lot for an AI to keep in its head at once, am I right? But the good news, the *solution* to that one-shot challenge, is right there in the second bullet point: the 'hierarchical structure of a screenplay.' Screenplays, themselves, are already broken down perfectly for an agentic flow. You've got the 'Premise,' which is just the basic elements of the movie, like, the title, the characters, the genre. Then, you build on that with the 'Story arc,' which is your high-level plot structure – your acts, your turning points. From there, you go into 'Sequences,' which are even more detailed plot points. And finally, you get down to the 'Scenes,' which is, like, the actual actions and dialogue happening in specific locations. So, what we're gonna do is build an AI agent for *each* of these levels, right? And that's how we're gonna defeat that 'one-shot' problem.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Screenplay Flow" in large, bold blue text. Below the title is a flowchart diagram illustrating the hierarchical structure of a screenplay, building on the concepts introduced on the previous slide. The flow starts with a blue rectangular box labeled "Premise" with the text "premise.json" below it. An arrow points from "Premise" to a purple rectangular box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json" below it. From "Arc," two arrows branch out: one points to an orange rectangular box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json," and the other points to a green rectangular box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json." Each of the four colored boxes also contains a small icon of a cardboard box robot, similar to the "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" robot from slide 1, suggesting an AI agent is involved in each step of this generation process. This diagram visually represents the agentic flow to generate screenplays, using the hierarchical elements of Premise, Arc, Sequences, and Scenes that were defined on slide 4, and indicating they produce JSON output at each stage.
slides.narration: Alright, so, this slide, 'Screenplay Flow,' this is it, people. This is the solution right here, visually represented. We just talked about how a screenplay has that natural hierarchical structure, right? Like, Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes. Well, we've basically mapped our AI agents directly to that structure. You can see each one of these colored boxes — blue 'Premise,' purple 'Arc,' orange 'Sequences,' green 'Scenes' — each of those represents a specialized AI agent, kinda like our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robots, right? They're working together in this flow. And notice, like, after each step, it produces a `.json` file. So, the 'Premise' agent, which, you know, handles all those basic elements of your movie, that spits out `premise.json`. Then, that `premise.json` feeds into the 'Arc' agent, which figures out your high-level plot structure, and that gives us `arc.json`. And from there, the 'Arc' feeds into both 'Sequences' and 'Scenes' (though usually sequences first, then scenes, but here it's showing both branching for simplicity). The 'Sequences' agent drills down into more detailed plot points, giving us `sequence.json`. And finally, the 'Scenes' agent gets into the nitty-gritty action and dialogue, generating `scenes.json`. So, yeah, this is our agentic workflow in action, breaking down that huge, overwhelming task of writing a full screenplay into these smaller, manageable, and very coherent steps. Pretty cool, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Premise Agent" in large, bold blue text, indicating the first stage of the screenplay generation process introduced on slide 5. In the top right corner, there is a small image of a cardboard box robot, similar to the "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" robot from slide 1 and the icons used in the flowchart on slide 5, visually representing the AI agent. Below the title are four bullet points. The first bullet describes the Premise Agent as "A chat agent that discusses with you what your movie is about." A sub-bullet specifies the elements discussed: "Title, logline (like an executive summary), characters, genre, central conflict," which elaborates on the 'Premise – basic elements of the movie' from slide 4. The second bullet states, "You can ask the agent to give ideas for the plot if you are stuck." The third bullet explains, "This chat can take a few minutes even if you have a general sense about your movie (takes time to specify all the major elements of the movie)." The final bullet point states, "This agent then creates a file premise.json," which aligns with the 'premise.json' output shown for the 'Premise' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on slide 5.
slides.narration: Alright, so, we just laid out that whole 'Screenplay Flow' with our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robots, right? Now, let's actually unpack each one of those agents, starting with the very first one: the 'Premise Agent.' So, you see our little robot up there, uh, this is your initial creative buddy. This is a chat agent, right? It's not just, like, passively taking inputs. You literally discuss with it what your movie is about. It's almost like having, you know, a brainstorming session with a super smart AI. You'll talk about the title, obviously, the logline – which, if you think about it, is kinda like an executive summary for your whole movie, like, what's the elevator pitch? – your main characters, the genre, what's the central conflict gonna be, all those really fundamental pieces, right? And the cool part is, if you're ever, like, stuck, you know, maybe you have a title but no plot, you can actually ask this agent to give *you* ideas for the plot. It's super helpful. Now, I will say, this chat, even if you have a pretty good idea of your movie, it can still take a few minutes. Because it's really important to specify all those major elements of the movie up front, you know? It's foundational. And then, once you're done with that conversation, this agent, it takes all that information and it creates a file called `premise.json`. That's your first output, and that's what's gonna feed into the next stage, which we'll talk about in a sec. Pretty cool, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold blue text, directly referencing the output file from the 'Premise Agent' discussed on slide 6 and the 'Premise' stage of the 'Screenplay Flow' on slide 5. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying a JSON object. This JSON object represents the content of the `premise.json` file, detailing the basic elements of a movie. It includes a "project_id" as "shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456" and a "title" as "Shadow of the Dark World," which are elements discussed by the Premise Agent. The "writers" field is set to "TBD," and "source_script" is null. "format_notes" states, "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat," linking to the 'A.W.E.S.O.M.-O' robot from slide 1 and the chat agent description from slide 6. The "logline" provides a summary: "A seemingly ordinary young man, Link, is thrust into a perilous quest after his uncle is gravely wounded rescuing Princess Zelda. He must gather legendary artifacts, wield the fabled Master Sword, and confront a malevolent wizard to prevent the dark lord Ganon's release and the conquest of their world," which is another element discussed by the Premise Agent. The "genre" is an array listing "fantasy," "action," and "adventure." Finally, "themes" is an array including "hero's journey," "destiny," "courage in the face of overwhelming evil," "good versus evil," and "sacrifice." These elements correspond to the 'title, logline, characters, genre, central conflict' mentioned for the Premise Agent on slide 6, and the 'Premise – basic elements of the movie' from slide 4.
slides.narration: Okay, so, now that we've talked about the Premise Agent, right? This is literally the output. This is 'Premise.json.' And you can see, this is that structured JSON file that that chat agent, you know, our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O buddy, generated after you chatted with it. So you've got your 'project_id' up top there, right? We've got our 'title,' 'Shadow of the Dark World.' You can see 'writers' is 'TBD' for now, 'source_script' is null. But look at 'format_notes': 'Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat.' Right? That's exactly what we talked about – you're talking to this thing in a chat, and it's spitting this out. And then, here's that 'logline' I mentioned, like the executive summary for your movie: 'A seemingly ordinary young man, Link, is thrust into a perilous quest after his uncle is gravely wounded rescuing Princess Zelda. He must gather legendary artifacts, wield the fabled Master Sword, and confront a malevolent wizard to prevent the dark lord Ganon's release and the conquest of their world.' Pretty epic, right? Like, all that from a chat. And then it's captured the 'genre' – fantasy, action, adventure. And the 'themes' – hero's journey, destiny, courage, good versus evil, sacrifice. All of this is, like, the fundamental DNA of our movie, captured in this nice, clean JSON. And this, right here, is what gets fed into the *next* agent. You know? Pretty cool.

slides.description: The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold blue text, indicating a continuation of the JSON output from the 'Premise Agent' discussed on slide 6. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying more content of the `premise.json` file. This segment details the 'central_conflict' as "Link's race against time to understand and embrace his role as a hero, stopping the sorcerer Aghanim from sacrificing Princess Zelda and six other maidens, a ritual that would unleash the ancient evil Ganon from the corrupted Dark World upon the Light World." It also defines the 'setting', which includes 'primary_time' as "High fantasy era, contemporary-feel for characters" and 'primary_places' as an array listing "Hyrule Castle," "Kakariko Village," "Eastern Palace," "Lost Woods," and "Dark World." Furthermore, it introduces 'protagonists' as an array containing an object for "Link," describing his 'name', 'role' as an "Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword," and his 'want' to "To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule." These fields provide further elaboration on the basic elements of the movie, such as the central conflict, characters, and setting, which were initiated on slide 7 and align with the 'Premise – basic elements of the movie' from slide 4.
slides.narration: Okay, so, we're still looking at our `premise.json`, right? We're just, like, getting deeper into the details that our Premise Agent hammered out. So, on this slide, you can see it's really clearly defined the 'central_conflict.' Like, Link's race against time to understand his role as a hero, stopping Aghanim from sacrificing Zelda and others to unleash Ganon. That's, you know, the whole shebang. And then, check out the 'setting.' We've got our 'primary_time' as a 'High fantasy era,' but with a 'contemporary-feel for characters,' which is a cool touch, I think. And then, the 'primary_places,' like, it's got all the classics: 'Hyrule Castle,' 'Kakariko Village,' 'Lost Woods,' 'Dark World' – you know, it's really grounding the story. And then, probably one of the most important parts for any movie, right? Your 'protagonists.' So, here's our main man, 'Link.' The AI has his 'name,' his 'role' as this 'Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword,' which is, you know, classic hero stuff. And his 'want' – 'To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule.' All that crucial character motivation and world-building, all from that initial chat with the Premise Agent. Pretty detailed, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold blue text, continuing to display the JSON output file from the 'Premise Agent' and 'Premise' stage of the screenplay flow. Below the title is a dark-themed code block showing more content of the `premise.json` file. This segment continues the 'protagonists' array, specifically showing the object for 'Link' with his 'name', 'role' as an "Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword," and 'want' to "To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule." Following this, it introduces a 'key_antagonist' object, detailing 'Aghanim' with his 'role' as an "Evil sorcerer, Ganon's pawn" and 'want' "To seal the seven maidens, including Princess Zelda, to open a portal to the Dark World and release Ganon." It also introduces a 'key_ally' object, defining 'Sahasrahla' with his 'role' as "Village elder, wise man, guide" and 'want' "To guide Link to acquire the Master Sword and stop Aghanim's plans to release Ganon." Finally, the JSON block includes 'stakes' as "The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon, resulting in the enslavement or destruction of its inhabitants." This slide elaborates further on the characters, their motivations, and the overarching stakes, all part of the basic elements of the movie discussed by the Premise Agent and started on slides 7 and 8.
slides.narration: Okay, so, we're still, like, really fleshing out our `premise.json`, right? Just getting all the crucial details down for 'Shadow of the Dark World.' We saw Link's info on the last slide, but it's good to see it again here, you know, our 'Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword,' and his clear 'want' to save everyone. But then, check this out: the AI has also generated our 'key_antagonist,' which is Aghanim. And it gives his 'role' as an 'Evil sorcerer, Ganon's pawn,' and his 'want' is super clear – 'To seal the seven maidens, including Princess Zelda, to open a portal to the Dark World and release Ganon.' So, you know, we know exactly what the bad guy's up to. And you need a good guide for your hero, right? So we have our 'key_ally,' Sahasrahla, the 'Village elder, wise man, guide,' whose 'want' is 'To guide Link to acquire the Master Sword and stop Aghanim's plans.' And finally, just to really set the stage, the 'stakes' are clearly defined here: 'The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon, resulting in the enslavement or destruction of its inhabitants.' I mean, you can't get much higher stakes than that! All of this, like, the characters, their motivations, the *entire* central conflict and stakes, it's all captured perfectly in this `premise.json` from that initial chat. It's the absolute bedrock for the rest of our screenplay, you know?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Arc Agent" in large, bold blue text, indicating the second stage of the screenplay generation process introduced on slide 5. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the cardboard box robot, similar to the "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" robot from slide 1 and the icons used in the flowchart on slide 5, visually representing this AI agent. Below the title are four bullet points. The first bullet describes the Arc Agent as "A chat agent that discusses your movie story arc." A sub-bullet clarifies, "Arc consists of multiple acts," which aligns with the 'Story arc – high level plot structure' from slide 4. Another sub-bullet specifies, "Agent takes premise.json as input for context," directly linking to the output of the Premise Agent detailed on slides 7-9. The second bullet explains the interaction model: "You tell the agent what happens in each act, then ask it to draft the act, one at a time." The third bullet reiterates, "Premise file gives it context for the story arc." The final bullet point states, "This agent then creates a file arc.json," which corresponds to the 'arc.json' output shown for the 'Arc' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on slide 5.
slides.narration: Alright, so we've got our `premise.json` all buttoned up, right? All the foundational stuff, characters, logline, stakes – it's all there, thanks to our first chat agent. Now, we're moving onto the *next* stage in our 'Screenplay Flow,' and that, my friends, is the 'Arc Agent.' You still see our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot up there, because guess what? This is *also* a chat agent, you know? It's still gonna be discussing things with you, but this time, it's all about your movie's 'story arc.' And as we talked about earlier, the arc, it consists of multiple acts, right? Like, Act 1, Act 2, Act 3, your high-level plot structure. Now, the super crucial thing here, that second sub-bullet, is that this agent, it *takes* that `premise.json` file we just created as its input for context. So it's not starting from scratch; it knows all about Link, Zelda, Ganon, the central conflict, everything. You don't have to re-explain it! So yeah, how you interact with it is pretty straightforward: you'll tell the agent what generally happens in each act, and then you ask it to draft that act for you, one at a time, you know? It's a guided process. The `premise.json` file, it just gives it that complete, like, global context for the story arc. And then, once you're done with that conversation, getting your acts all dialed in, this agent, it creates a new file for us, called `arc.json`. And that's gonna be our next big output, which we'll look at right after this. Pretty cool how it all just builds, right?

slides.description:
The slide is titled "Arc.json" in large, bold blue text. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying a JSON object, representing the content of the `arc.json` file. This file is the output of the Arc Agent discussed on slide 10 and corresponds to the 'Arc' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" from slide 5.

The JSON object details 'act: 3' of the movie's story arc. It includes a "label" as "Battle Aghanim and the Dark World" and an "approx_pages_note" indicating this act, encompassing the climax and a major cliffhanger, will be around 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay. The "dramatic_question" poses, "Can Link defeat Aghanim, save Zelda and the other maidens, and prevent Ganon from conquering their world, even when cast into the heart of darkness?", directly relating to the central conflict and stakes established in the `premise.json` on slides 8-9.

A "summary" provides a detailed narrative of the act, describing Link finding the Master Sword, Zelda's capture, Aghanim's ritual to release Ganon, Link's battles, and Sahasrahla's guidance, all consistent with the movie's premise (slides 7-9). The "turning_point_end" is described as "Aghanim banishes Link to the corrupted Dark World, setting the stage for a new, desperate quest as the ultimate evil, Ganon, looms large."

An array of "must_hit_beats" outlines key plot points within Act 3. These beats include Sahasrahla guiding Link to the Master Sword in the Lost Woods, Link activating and pulling the sword from the stone, Zelda telepathically contacting Link after being captured, Link's return to Hyrule Castle to battle enchanted soldiers, his confrontation with Aghanim who zaps Zelda away after gathering seven maidens, Aghanim revealing his plan to unleash Ganon, Link battling and wounding Aghanim with the Master Sword, Aghanim banishing Link to the Dark World, Link landing on the Great Pyramid, and Sahasrahla instructing Link to rescue the seven maidens to break the barrier to Ganon's tower. The final field, "midpoint_note", is null.
slides.narration: Alright, so we've had our chat with the Arc Agent, right? We talked through the high-level plot, the acts, what generally needs to happen. And this right here, this is the output: `arc.json`. Just like with `premise.json`, everything is perfectly structured. Now, what you're seeing here is specifically Act 3, you know? The big finale, with a major cliffhanger. And look, it even gives us an `approx_pages_note`, saying this act is gonna be, like, 20-30 pages of our 120-page screenplay. That's pretty useful for pacing, right? Then, you've got the `dramatic_question` for this act, which, you know, really hones in on the central conflict we established in the `premise.json`: 'Can Link defeat Aghanim, save Zelda and the other maidens, and prevent Ganon from conquering their world, even when cast into the heart of darkness?' Like, super high stakes, am I right? And then, we've got this awesome `summary` that just lays out the entire act, from Link finding the Master Sword, to Zelda's capture, Aghanim's ritual, Link's battles, Sahasrahla guiding him – all perfectly consistent with our premise. And then, the `turning_point_end` for this act is huge: 'Aghanim banishes Link to the corrupted Dark World, setting the stage for a new, desperate quest as the ultimate evil, Ganon, looms large.' Talk about a cliffhanger! But the *really* cool part, like, the detail, is in these `must_hit_beats`. This is where the AI breaks down all those critical plot points that *have* to happen in Act 3: Sahasrahla guiding Link to the Master Sword, Link pulling it from the stone, Zelda's telepathic contact, the battle in Hyrule Castle, the confrontation with Aghanim, Aghanim banishing Link to the Dark World... it's all just laid out. Every single, like, essential narrative beat, right there. This is what makes sure our story stays coherent and on track, even with all these complex plot points. And this, this perfectly structured `arc.json`, this is what's gonna feed into our *next* set of agents.

slides.description:
The slide is titled "Sequence Agent" in large, bold blue text, indicating the next stage in the screenplay generation process from the "Screenplay Flow" introduced on slide 5. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the cardboard box robot (A.W.E.S.O.M.-O), visually representing this AI agent. Below the title are four bullet points.
The first bullet describes the Sequence Agent as "An automated agent (no chat) that writes the sequences of the movie for each act," distinguishing it from the previous chat-based agents and aligning with the 'Sequences – more detailed plot' from slide 4.
The second main bullet, "Inputs per sequence," details the information the agent uses. A sub-bullet states, "Premise.json, arc.json – give agent global view of the movie," indicating it uses the output from the Premise Agent (slides 7-9) and the Arc Agent (slide 11) for context. Another sub-bullet specifies, "All previous sequences generated so far – give agent local context for what should happen next," suggesting an iterative process.
The third main bullet, "Primary sequence elements," outlines what the agent focuses on generating for each sequence. It lists three sub-points: "Goal – what is the narrative purpose of this sequence," "Key locations – locations in the sequence," and "Exit beat – how the sequence ends and transitions to next sequence."
The final bullet point states, "This agent then creates a file sequence.json," which corresponds to the 'sequence.json' output shown for the 'Sequences' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on slide 5.
slides.narration: Alright, so we've got our `premise.json` and our `arc.json` all sorted, right? We've chatted with A.W.E.S.O.M.-O, figured out the basic elements and the high-level plot. Now, we're moving on to the 'Sequence Agent,' and this is where things get a little different. Notice that first bullet point: this is an *automated* agent, people. No more chat! It's not going to sit there and brainstorm with you. Its job is to just take all that beautiful JSON we've created and, like, get to work, writing the detailed sequences of the movie for each act. So, how does it do that? Well, for its 'Inputs per sequence,' it's super smart, right? It takes both `premise.json` and `arc.json`, so it has that 'global view of the movie,' you know? It knows the whole story, the characters, the stakes, the acts. But here's the cool part for 'local context': it also takes 'All previous sequences generated so far.' This is how it maintains coherence, right? It knows what just happened, so it can make sure what happens *next* makes sense. And what does it produce? For each sequence, it focuses on these 'Primary sequence elements': the 'Goal' – like, what's the narrative purpose of this particular sequence? What needs to be achieved? The 'Key locations' – where is this sequence happening? And then the 'Exit beat' – how does this sequence end, and how does it transition smoothly into the *next* sequence? It's all about keeping that flow. And then, once it's done its magic, this agent creates a file called `sequence.json`. That's our next big output, moving us even closer to a full screenplay. Pretty efficient, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Sequence.json" in large, bold blue text, directly referencing the output file from the 'Sequence Agent' discussed on slide 12 and the 'Sequences' stage of the 'Screenplay Flow' on slide 5. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying a JSON object, representing part of the content of the `sequence.json` file. This JSON object begins with a "project_id" as "shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456", consistent with the `premise.json` file. It indicates a "sequence_count" of 8 and a null "note" field. The core of the object is a "sequences" array, of which the first sequence, identified by "id": "SEQ1", is fully displayed. This sequence is for "act": 1 and has a "title" of "Zelda's Urgent Plea." Its "goal" is described as "To establish Link's call to adventure, his initial motivation (saving his uncle and Zelda), and to equip him with his first tools for the perilous journey ahead," aligning with the 'Goal' element mentioned on slide 12. The "emotional_tone" is set to "Urgent, sorrowful, determined." The "key_locations" array includes "Link's Home" and "Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)," corresponding to the 'Key locations' element from slide 12. Finally, the "exit_beat" states, "Armed with his uncle's sword and shield, Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle and rescue Princess Zelda," which matches the 'Exit beat' element from slide 12. The JSON structure indicates that additional sequences would follow, but they are not visible on this slide.
slides.narration: Alright, so after that automated Sequence Agent has done its thing, crunching through our `premise.json` and `arc.json`, this is what we get: `sequence.json`! This is where we start seeing the movie really, like, breaking down into more granular chunks. You can see our familiar `project_id` up top there, right? Consistent with our premise. And then, it tells us the `sequence_count` is 8, so there are eight of these bad boys for our whole movie. And what you're seeing here is `SEQ1`, the very first sequence. It's for Act 1, obviously, and it's got this awesome title: 'Zelda's Urgent Plea.' And look at the `goal` for this sequence, right? It's 'To establish Link's call to adventure, his initial motivation (saving his uncle and Zelda), and to equip him with his first tools for the perilous journey ahead.' That's super clear, you know? And the `emotional_tone` is 'Urgent, sorrowful, determined' – perfectly setting the mood. Then it gives us the `key_locations`: 'Link's Home' and 'Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls).' And finally, the `exit_beat` for this sequence: 'Armed with his uncle's sword and shield, Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle and rescue Princess Zelda.' So you can see, like, how detailed this is, and how it perfectly sets up the *next* logical step in the story. It's all just building, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Scene Agent" in large, bold blue text, indicating the final stage in the screenplay generation process from the "Screenplay Flow" introduced on slide 5. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the cardboard box robot (A.W.E.S.O.M.-O), visually representing this AI agent. Below the title are four bullet points. The first bullet describes the Scene Agent as "An automated agent (no chat) that writes the scenes of the movie for each sequence," distinguishing it as an automated process like the Sequence Agent on slide 12, and aligning with the 'Scenes – even more detailed plot' from slide 4. The second main bullet, "Inputs per sequence," details the information the agent uses. A sub-bullet states, "Premise.json, arc.json, sequence.json – give agent global view of the movie," indicating it uses the output from the Premise Agent (slides 7-9), the Arc Agent (slide 11), and the Sequence Agent (slide 13) for overall context. Another sub-bullet specifies, "All scenes generated so far in this sequence and the previous sequence – give agent local context for what should happen next," suggesting an iterative generation process that builds on prior scene content. The third main bullet, "Primary scene elements," outlines what the agent focuses on generating for each scene. It lists two sub-points: "Slug line – location of the scene" and "Blocks – series of actions and dialogues in the scene," which provide concrete elements for 'Scene action and dialogue' mentioned on slide 4. The final bullet point states, "This agent then creates a file scenes.json," which directly corresponds to the 'scenes.json' output shown for the 'Scenes' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on slide 5.
slides.narration: Alright, alright, alright. So we've gone from the big picture with the Premise, then the high-level plot with the Arc, then the detailed sequences. Now, this is it, people, we're diving into the absolute nitty-gritty: the 'Scene Agent.' And yep, you see our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot up there, ready to work. Just like the Sequence Agent, this one is *fully automated*, you know? No more chatting! Its job is to just take all that beautiful JSON we've built up and, like, write the actual scenes of the movie for each sequence. So, what does this agent need to do its magic? For its 'Inputs per sequence,' it's super smart, right? It's taking the `premise.json`, the `arc.json`, *and* the `sequence.json` – that gives it, like, the total 'global view of the movie.' It knows everything that's supposed to happen. But here's the really crucial part for maintaining coherence and avoiding that 'context drift' we talked about: it also takes 'All scenes generated so far in this sequence and the previous sequence.' This is how it keeps that 'local context' tight, ensuring what it writes *next* makes perfect sense with what just happened. And then, for what it actually generates, its 'Primary scene elements' are, like, the fundamentals of a script: the 'Slug line' – which is, you know, the location of the scene, like 'INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT' – and then the 'Blocks,' which are all the series of actions and dialogues in that scene. This is where, like, the rubber meets the road. And finally, once it's done cranking all that out, this agent creates our final output for the core flow: a file called `scenes.json`. That's where you'll find your actual screenplay, scene by scene. Pretty cool, huh?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Scenes.json" in large, bold blue text, directly referencing the output file from the 'Scene Agent' discussed on slide 14 and the 'Scenes' stage of the 'Screenplay Flow' on slide 5. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying a JSON object, representing a portion of the content of the `scenes.json` file. The root of the JSON is a "scenes" array, containing an object for the first scene. This scene has an "id" of "S0001", a "slug_id" of "SEQ1-01" indicating it's the first scene of Sequence 1, and a "slugline" as "INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT", which corresponds to the 'Slug line' element described for the Scene Agent on slide 14. The "line_start" field is null, and the "sequence_id" is "SEQ1". The core of the scene is presented in a "blocks" array, which aligns with the 'Blocks' element from slide 14, detailing actions and dialogues. The blocks describe Link sleeping in his home, a desperate whisper from ZELDA (V.O.) echoing in his mind saying "Link... help me... I'm trapped in the castle... Aghanim... has me...", Link jolting awake, and then clutching his head as he sees visions of Hyrule Castle, Princess Zelda's terrified face, and the menacing silhouette of a sorcerer. This narrative content directly builds upon the movie's 'Premise' (slides 7-9), 'Arc' (slide 11), and specifically 'Sequence 1: Zelda's Urgent Plea' (slide 13), establishing Link's call to adventure and the central conflict with Aghanim.
slides.narration: Alright, so after all that, the Premise, the Arc, the Sequences, we've finally made it to the actual, like, screenplay, right? This is our `scenes.json` output, the culmination of that entire core agentic flow. You can see it's a 'scenes' array, and what we're looking at here is the very first scene. So, it's got its `id` as 'S0001', and check out that `slug_id`: 'SEQ1-01', which tells us, yeah, this is the first scene of our first sequence, 'Zelda's Urgent Plea.' And then, obviously, the 'slugline' is 'INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT', setting our stage. Now, the really cool part, the actual writing, is in this 'blocks' array. This is where the magic happens, you know? It kicks off with Link, our 'unassuming hero,' just peacefully sleeping in his simple home. But then, BAM! 'Suddenly, a piercing, desperate WHISPER echoes in his mind...' And who is it? It's ZELDA (V.O.)! 'Link... help me... I'm trapped in the castle... Aghanim... has me...' I mean, talk about a dramatic wake-up call, right? He jolts awake, disoriented, and then clutches his head as visions flash through his mind: 'the cold stone walls of HYRULE CASTLE, Princess ZELDA'S terrified face, the menacing silhouette of a SORCERER.' So yeah, this scene perfectly establishes Link's call to adventure, the central conflict with Aghanim, and Zelda's immediate peril, directly building on everything we hammered out in the `premise.json`, `arc.json`, and our `sequence.json`. This is the proof, people, that breaking it down hierarchically actually *works* to create a coherent, detailed narrative. Pretty epic, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled "Enhancement Agents" in large, bold blue text. This title introduces a new type of agent in the screenplay generation process, building on the Premise, Arc, Sequence, and Scene Agents from previous slides. Below the title is a bullet point that states, "We can improve the agentic flow by using agents to enhance intermediate outputs." This bullet has three sub-points: "– Write better sequences or scenes," "– Clean up the premise," and "– Make the arc more creative." To the right of the bullet points is an image of a golden cardboard box robot, similar to the 'A.W.E.S.O.M.-O' robot from slide 1, with a label on its chest that reads "ENHANCEMENT AGENT." Below the text and the robot image is a flowchart diagram. This diagram is a modified version of the "Screenplay Flow" seen on slide 5. It shows three of the original rectangular boxes: a blue "Premise" box with "premise.json," a purple "Arc" box with "arc.json," and an orange "Sequences" box with "sequence.json." A green "Scenes" box with "scenes.json" is positioned below the Arc box. Unlike the original flow on slide 5 where arrows directly connected the main agent boxes, this diagram now features small images of the cardboard box robot (similar to the 'A.W.E.S.O.M.-O' and the Enhancement Agent robot) positioned between the boxes. An arrow points from the 'Premise' box to one of these robot images, which then points to the 'Arc' box. Another arrow points from the 'Arc' box to a robot image, which then points to the 'Sequences' box. A separate arrow points from the 'Arc' box to another robot image, which then points to the 'Scenes' box. Each of the main boxes (Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes) also contains a small icon of the cardboard box robot, indicating their role as agents. The placement of the robot images between the main stages suggests that these 'Enhancement Agents' can operate on or between the outputs of the primary agents (Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes) to refine them, as described in the bullet points.
slides.narration: Okay, so, we've gone through the whole core flow, right? Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes – our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robots are doing their thing, spitting out `json` files at each stage. But what if we want to make it *even better*? Like, what if the first draft of our `premise.json` could be, I don't know, cleaned up a bit? Or maybe the sequences could be, like, more dramatic? Well, that's where these guys come in: 'Enhancement Agents.' You see that shiny golden A.W.E.S.O.M.-O up there with 'ENHANCEMENT AGENT' on his chest? These are, like, our quality control agents, right? The idea is, we can actually improve this entire agentic flow by having *other* agents whose sole job is to enhance those *intermediate outputs*. So, after the Premise Agent spits out `premise.json`, maybe an Enhancement Agent steps in to, you know, 'clean up the premise' a little, make sure all the elements are super tight. Or, after the Arc Agent gives us `arc.json`, another Enhancement Agent could jump in to 'make the arc more creative,' right? Like, give it a bit more oomph, a bit more flair. And then, down the line, these agents can also 'write better sequences or scenes.' And if you look at the diagram down there, you can see how it's integrated. Those little robots *between* the main blue 'Premise' and purple 'Arc' boxes, and between 'Arc' and 'Sequences,' and 'Arc' and 'Scenes' – those are our Enhancement Agents. They're basically, like, intercepting the `json` output from one stage, refining it, and *then* passing that improved `json` onto the *next* primary agent. So it's not just a linear flow; it's a constantly improving, iterative process. Pretty smart, right? It's how we ensure the final output is top-tier.

slides.description:
The slide is titled "Generalizing to Other Documents" in large, bold blue text, directly addressing the concept introduced on slide 2 about generalizing the agentic flow. Below the title is a bullet point that states, "By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents," reinforcing that the multi-stage AI generation process, like the 'Screenplay Flow' introduced on slide 5, can be adapted.

The main content of the slide is a table with six columns: "Document," "Premise," "Arc," "Sequence," "Scene," and "Artist Rendering." This table illustrates how the hierarchical structure used for screenplays (Premise, Arc, Sequence, Scene) can be applied to different types of documents:

*   For a **Book**: The 'Premise' maps to "Premise," 'Arc' to "Table of contents," 'Sequence' to "Chapter," and 'Scene' to "Sub-chapter."
*   For a **Podcast**: The 'Premise' maps to "Concept," 'Arc' to "Flow, format," 'Sequence' to "Segment," and 'Scene' to "Beat."
*   For a **Song**: The 'Premise' maps to "Premise," 'Arc' to "Structure (verse, hooks, coda)," 'Sequence' to "Verse," and 'Scene' to "Couplet."
*   For a **Lecture**: The 'Premise' maps to "Learning objective," 'Arc' to "Outline," 'Sequence' to "Module," and 'Scene' to "Slide."
*   For a **Business plan**: The 'Premise' maps to "Value prop," 'Arc' to "Outline," 'Sequence' to "Section," and 'Scene' to "Sub-section."

The "Artist Rendering" column for each document type displays a small illustrative image featuring the cardboard box robot, similar to the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot from slide 1, depicted in a setting relevant to the document type (e.g., in a library for a book, a recording studio for a podcast, a stage for a song, a lecture hall for a lecture, and an office for a business plan), visually representing the AI agent's role in generating these diverse documents.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've seen how we can use 'Enhancement Agents' to make our screenplays even better, right? It's all about refining those intermediate outputs. But here's the *really* cool part, and this is what we hinted at all the way back on slide two: 'Generalizing to Other Documents.' The power of this hierarchical flow, with our specialized agents, is that it's not just for screenplays, you know? By simply modifying the agent prompts – like, telling the Premise Agent, 'Hey, instead of a movie premise, I need a book premise,' or the Arc Agent, 'Give me a table of contents instead of a story arc' – this exact same architecture can be used for, like, *any* long text document you can imagine. Look at this table! For a 'Book,' your Premise is still a 'Premise,' but your Arc becomes a 'Table of contents,' your Sequence is a 'Chapter,' and your Scene is a 'Sub-chapter.' Pretty neat, right? Or for a 'Podcast,' your Premise is the 'Concept,' the Arc is the 'Flow, format,' the Sequence is a 'Segment,' and the Scene is a 'Beat' within that segment. Think about a 'Song'! Premise is the 'Premise' of the song, the Arc is the 'Structure' – like, verse, hooks, coda – the Sequence becomes a 'Verse,' and the Scene is a 'Couplet.' And get this: even for a 'Lecture,' like the one I'm giving right now! The Premise is your 'Learning objective,' the Arc is the 'Outline,' the Sequence is a 'Module,' and guess what the Scene is? It's a 'Slide.' Right? Like, this very slide is a 'Scene' in our lecture flow. And then for a 'Business plan,' your Premise is your 'Value prop,' your Arc is your 'Outline,' the Sequence is a 'Section,' and the Scene is a 'Sub-section.' And you can see in the 'Artist Rendering' column, our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot is there, like, in a library for a book, or in a recording studio for a podcast, in a lecture hall for a lecture. It's just showing you, like, how versatile and powerful this architecture is. It's not limited to one domain, you know? It's all about adapting those prompts. Super cool stuff.

slides.description:
The slide is titled "Coding Session" in large, bold blue text. Below the title are three bullet points. The first bullet states, "Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O," referring to the AI robot and the main topic of the presentation. The second bullet states, "Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow," which directly relates to the concept of Enhancement Agents introduced on slide 16. The third bullet states, "Modify Awesome-O for different document types," referencing the generalization of the AI agent for various long text documents discussed on slide 17.

Below the bullet points is a flowchart diagram that is identical to the modified "Screenplay Flow" shown on slide 16, illustrating the inclusion of enhancement agents. The flow starts with a blue rectangular box labeled "Premise" with "premise.json" below it. An arrow points from "Premise" to a small image of a cardboard box robot, which then points to a purple rectangular box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json." From "Arc," two arrows branch out: one points to a cardboard box robot image, which then points to an orange rectangular box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json." The other arrow from "Arc" points to a different cardboard box robot image, which then points to a green rectangular box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json." Each of the four colored boxes (Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes) also contains a small icon of a cardboard box robot, similar to the "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" robot from slide 1, representing the agent for each stage. The presence of the robot images between the main stages reinforces the idea of enhancement agents operating on or between the outputs of the primary agents.
slides.narration: Alright, alright, alright. So, we've walked through the entire 'Screenplay Flow,' right? From the Premise Agent all the way down to the Scene Agent, and we've talked about how we can make it even better with 'Enhancement Agents,' and how this whole thing is super flexible for 'Generalizing to Other Documents.' Now, this slide, 'Coding Session,' this is where we actually get our hands dirty, people! This is the good stuff. You see those three bullet points? That's our mission for this session. First, we're gonna 'Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O.' You know, bring our little robot buddy to life and actually generate some scenes. Then, we're gonna 'Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow,' just like that diagram shows down there, right? We'll see how those little robots in between the main stages actually refine and improve our outputs. And finally, the super cool part: we're gonna 'Modify Awesome-O for different document types.' We'll actually, like, tweak the prompts and see how this exact same architecture can generate something totally different, maybe a lecture outline, or a podcast segment. So yeah, we're gonna take everything we've talked about in theory and put it into practice. This is gonna be fun, I think. Let's dive into the code!
